coredata.dc:description: Electrical stimulation from wearable triboelectric nanogenerators is promising for wound treatment, but has limitations in healing infected wounds due to its restricted bactericidal efficacy. While combining triboelectric nanogenerators with antibacterial drugs is an effective approach, it faces challenges such as drug-loading content, patient compliance, and biosafety. Here, a wearable, self-powered microneedle system based on conductive drugs is developed for infected wounds through antimicrobial treatment and electrically stimulated tissue repair, in which the electrical stimulation is conducted by the designed drugs. The drug is synthesized through covalent bonding between levofloxacin and carbon quantum dots, eliminating bacteria effectively and endowing the microneedle patch with excellent conductivity. The electrical stimulation from a triboelectric nanogenerator delivered to the wound tissue promotes cell migration, cell proliferation, and collagen deposition. Infected-wound mice models demonstrate that the synergistic action of drugs and electrical stimulation in this system is more effective in promoting the healing of wounds than the sole application of either electrical stimulation or drugs. This study presents an innovative strategy for the treatment of infected wounds and paves the way for new approaches in wearable electronic devices for wound therapy.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The SepMN system for accelerating the healing of infected wounds. (A) Schematic illustration showing possible practical applications of the SepMN system. (B) Configuration of the SepMN system for the combined delivery of electrical stimulation and antibacterial LevCDs. (C) TEM (i) and HRTEM (ii) images of LevCDs. The scale bar is 20 nm. (D) Photographs of the LevMN in daylight (i) and UV light (ii). The scale bars are 500 μm in (i) and 200 μm in (ii). (E) SepMN system for treating infected wounds in mice. The inset is a photograph of inside the bandage. (F) The mechanism of the SepMN system for treating infected wounds.

Physical and chemical characterization of LevCDs. (A) Schematic diagram of the preparation process of LevCDs. (B) The conductivity of LevCDs at different concentrations. (C) FTIR spectra of AnCDs and LevCDs. (D) N1s XPS spectra of AnCDs and LevCDs. (E) Fluorescence-emission spectra of AnCDs and LevCDs.

The triboelectric output performance of the SepMN system. (A) Tensile properties of a flexible triboelectric electrode (FTE). Scale bars are 1 cm. (B) Mechanical compression–force curves of LevMN. A photograph of mouse skin after LevMN compression is shown in the inset. (C) Fabrics that generated high voltages with the FTE: (i) tropical suiting, (ii) knitting wool, (iii) polyester fiber, (iv) PTFE fiberglass, (v) imitation silk, and (vi) acetate fiber. (D) Fabrics that generated low voltages with the FTE: (i) alpaca wool, (ii) doupioni pongee, (iii) DuPont paper, and (iv) mercerized cotton. (E) Triboelectric working mechanism of SepMN system. (F, G) Voltage- and current-response characteristics (respectively) of a TENG composed of the FTE and PTFE fiberglass. (H) Dependence of the voltage output and current of the TENG on the resistive load. (I) Voltage output of the TENG during 400,000 contact–separation working cycles. (J) Activation of 60 LEDs through manual patting of the FTE and PTFE fiberglass with the LevMN as the wire. The scale bars are 4 cm in the upper image and 50 μm in the lower two images.

Biocompatibility of SepMN system and the in vitro cellular responses subjected to LevCDs or electrical stimulation. (A) Relative cell viability of NIH3T3 cells cultured on different groups and corresponding fluorescence images (B). Scale bars are 100 μm. (C) Hemolysis ratios treated by different groups and corresponding optical images of red blood cells (D). Scale bars are 50 μm. (E) Cell proliferation ratio of various groups. (F) Cell migration images of various groups and corresponding cell migration ratio (G). Scale bars are 200 µm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Antibacterial activity of SepMN system. Images of bacteria-infected cells treated by different methods. Scale bars are 200 μm. (B) The bacteria survival rate after sole electrical stimulation generated by the TENG treatment for 2 h. (C) Photographs of the colonies in agar plates after different treatments. The scale bars are 10 mm. (D, E) Survival rates of S. aureus and E. coli, respectively, in different groups; the residual CFUs are shown in the insets. (F) Photographs of the inhibition zone generated by different groups. The scale bars are 10 mm. (G) Larger diameter of the inhibition zone for the SepMN than for the LevMN.

In vivo evaluation of SepMN system in mice models of infected wounds. (A) Experimental steps for treating infected wounds with the SepMN system. (B) Schematic illustration of the establishment and treatment of the infected wound. (C) Photographs of the wound-healing process on days 0, 3, 6, 9, and 12. Scale bars are 2 mm. (D) Relative wound area of different groups within 12 days. (E) Tissue slices were stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) and Masson’s trichrome for different groups on day 12. Scale bars are 500 μm. Quantitative analysis of granulation tissue width (F) and epithelial tissue thickness (G).

head.ce:title: A wearable self-powered microneedle system based on conductive drugs for infected wound healing: A new electrical stimulation delivery strategy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Electrical stimulation from wearable triboelectric nanogenerators is promising for wound treatment, but has limitations in healing infected wounds due to its restricted bactericidal efficacy. While combining triboelectric nanogenerators with antibacterial drugs is an effective approach, it faces challenges such as drug-loading content, patient compliance, and biosafety. Here, a wearable, self-powered microneedle system based on conductive drugs is developed for infected wounds through antimicrobial treatment and electrically stimulated tissue repair, in which the electrical stimulation is conducted by the designed drugs. The drug is synthesized through covalent bonding between levofloxacin and carbon quantum dots, eliminating bacteria effectively and endowing the microneedle patch with excellent conductivity. The electrical stimulation from a triboelectric nanogenerator delivered to the wound tissue promotes cell migration, cell proliferation, and collagen deposition. Infected-wound mice models demonstrate that the synergistic action of drugs and electrical stimulation in this system is more effective in promoting the healing of wounds than the sole application of either electrical stimulation or drugs. This study presents an innovative strategy for the treatment of infected wounds and paves the way for new approaches in wearable electronic devices for wound therapy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As the body’s most extensive organ, the skin has a crucial role in shielding the body from pathogens [1,2]. Injuries caused by accidents or diseases can compromise the skin’s defensive functions, sometimes leading to serious health outcomes. Several advanced therapeutic strategies have been implemented for accelerating wound healing, including hyperbaric oxygen therapy [3], photothermal therapy [4], negative pressure therapy [5], stem cell therapy [6], and electrical stimulation therapy [7,8]. Among these different strategies, electrical stimulation has shown particular promise for expediting wound closure and improving the overall quality of wound healing by encouraging collagen deposition, improving cell activity, and reducing oedema [9–11]. However, the current clinical electrical stimulation equipment is bulky, expensive, and cannot be used in places with inadequate energy supply [12,13]. As a result, the development of miniaturized, lightweight, self-powered, and wearable devices for wound healing is necessary.

Triboelectric nanogenerators (TENGs) have an excellent capacity for harnessing low-frequency kinetic energy and enabling the miniaturization of electrical stimulation devices [14,15]. The electrical stimulation produced by TENGs has been shown to effectively expedite non-infectious wound healing [16,17]. However, bacterial proliferation within wounds can hinder the healing process through continuous inflammation, damage to new tissue, nutrient depletion, and the potential formation of obstructive biofilms [18,19]. Several studies have investigated the antibacterial activity of electrical stimulation generated by TENGs. For example, in one study that used aluminum sheets as electrodes to treat bacteria at 8 V for 24 h, the survival rate of bacteria remained close to 40 % [20]. In another study, a low voltage of 2 V applied on carbon fabric coated with nanowires generated a strong electric field at the tips of the nanowires, which eliminated bacteria effectively [21]. In addition, thermally catalyzed bismuth telluride nanoplates on carbon fabric electrodes triggered by the surrounding temperature were reported to produce hydrogen peroxide for bacteria inhibition [22]. However, these studies have limitations in terms of antibacterial efficiency, electrode strength, and the conditions required to trigger antimicrobial activity. Because antibacterial drugs have the advantages of high efficiency and biosafety for bacterial eradication [23,24]. the synergistic use of electrical stimulation and drugs to treat infected wounds is a promising strategy [20,25].

It is pertinent to note that electrodes should deliver both electrical stimulation and drugs in this combined strategy. Metals [20] and metal-sputtered polymer materials [26] are commonly used as electrodes, in which the drugs can only be affixed to the surface. This significantly limits their drug-loading capacity. Moreover, the use of metal electrodes in the wound site may affect patient comfort. The ion-conducting materials (e.g., LiCl) [16] within polymer materials can also serve as electrodes. However, these materials (along with the drugs) could be released into the wound, potentially causing biosafety issues. In addition, the type of drug-loaded electrode has a strong influence on the efficiency of drug delivery and the therapeutic effect of electrical stimulation. Compared to common electrodes contact with the wound, microneedle patches have emerged as a more effective approach for administering a stable, constant drug concentration and for the precise delivery of electrical stimulation into the wound [27,28].

In this study, we developed a wearable, self-powered microneedle (SepMN) system based on conductive drugs for treating infected wounds through antimicrobial treatment and electrically stimulated tissue repair. Our system has three novel advantages over traditional approaches. First, a novel conductive drug (LevCDs) is synthesized with excellent antimicrobial efficacy and electrical conductivity through the covalent bonding of levofloxacin (Lev) and carbon quantum dots (CDs). Second, the LevCDs-loaded microneedle patch (LevMN) allows for the delivery of electrical stimulation and LevCDs simultaneously to the infected wounds. Finally, the separate design of TENG and LevMN can avoid the secondary injury of the wound and collect energy of motion effectively. By loading LevCDs into the microneedle patches, a combination effect of antimicrobial treatment and electrically stimulated tissue repair in infected wounds was achieved, solving the problems of drug-loading capacity, patient compliance, and biosafety in traditional methods. The preparation, properties, and wound-treatment applications of the SepMN system were comprehensively evaluated in vitro and in vivo.

Glucose (AR), ammonia (25 % solution), sodium chloride (NaCl, AR), sodium citrate (98 %), glycerol (AR), and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 %) were provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. (Shanghai, China). N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 98 %), 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC-HCl, 98 %), sodium alginate (Viscosity 200 ± 20 mpa.s), acrylamide (AAm, AR), N,N-methylenebisacrylamide (MBAA, 99 %) and levofloxacin (98.0 %) were purchased from Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co. (Shanghai, China). Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA, 99 %) was obtained from Energy-Chemical. Mxene (Ti3C2) was bought from FoShan Xinxi TechnologyCo., Ltd.(FoShan, China). Polyethylene glycol diacrylate (PEGDA, average Mn 700) and 2-Hydroxy-2-methylpropiophenone (HMPP, 97 %) were from Sigma-Aldrich. Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) was purchased from Selleck. All reagents were used without any purification. The microneedle mold was purchased from Henan Micro-Nano BenTeng Biotechnology Co., Ltd (Henan, China). Different fabrics were provided by Qixiu Fabric and then fabricated to 0.4 mm uniformly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell lines, bacteria strain, and animals
ce:section.ce:para: NIH 3 T3 cell lines were bought from ATCC (America). The bacteria strain was bought from the Beijing Microbiological Culture Collection Center (China). The 10-week-old male BALBc mice were supplied by Changsheng Biotechnology (China).

Alginate (2 wt%, 60 %v/v), Mxene (5 mg/mL, 35 %v/v), and glycerol (5 %v/v) were stirred homogeneously at room temperature. It was poured into a Teflon mold and dried in a draught drying cabinet at 60 °C for 4 h to obtain a flexible membrane. The membrane was removed from the mold and cross-linked with Ca2+ (10 wt%) for 12 s to obtain the expected membrane as the triboelectric electrode of TENG. A crank linkage mechanism driven by a motor was used to provide vertical force between the FTE and the common clothing fabric to get the electrical performance of the TENG.

AnCDs were first synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method using glucose as the carbon source and ammonia as the nitrogen source. In detail, ammonia (5 mL) and hydrogen peroxide (2 mL) were added to glucose aqueous solution (20 mL, 10 mg/mL). After the mixture was stirred for 30 min, it was put into a Teflon-lined autoclave (50 mL) and heated at 180 ℃ for 4 h. After natural cooling to room temperature, the obtained solution was passed through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane filter and dialyzed against DI water using a dialysis bag (1000 Da, Spectrum Lab. Inc) for 12 h, and the AnCDs could be obtained after fry-drying. Then, the LevCDs were fabricated by the conjunction of levofloxacin onto the surface of AnCDs. Specifically, levofloxacin was dissolved in a PBS buffer solution at a concentration of 5 mg/mL. Then, twice the molar mass of EDC-HCl and NHS than levofloxacin above were used to activate its carboxyl group in a 37 °C water bath for half an hour. Next, the AnCDs solution (10 mL, 5 mg/mL) was mixed with the activated levofloxacin solution (5 mL) and co-cultured at room temperature for 24 h. After that, LevCDs could be obtained by filtration, dialysis, and freeze-drying.

The LevMN patch was fabricated using the PEGDA-enhanced classical PAAm-alginate composition. As shown in Fig. S10, alginate (1 wt%), AAm (18 wt%), MBAA (0.054 wt%), LevCDs (2 mg/mL), PEGDA (25 % v/v), and HMPP (1 % v/v) were mixed homogeneously as the pre-gel. Then, the pre-gel (300 μL) was added to the microneedle mold, and the LevMN could be obtained after vacuuming, UV light curing, and washing.

The nanostructures of AnCDs and LevCDs were measured by a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2010HR). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra was characterized by an FT-IR spectrophotometer (TA Instruments, TGA-Q500, New Castle, DE, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; Thermo Fisher) and fluorescent spectrometer (HORIBA Jobin Yvon) were also employed to examine the different properties between AnCDs and LevCDs. The morphology and elemental distribution of FTE and LevMN were characterized using scanning electron microscopy (SEM, SU-8010, HITACHI, Ibaraki, Japan) equipped with EDX. The electrical signal of TENG was measured using an electrometer (Keithley 6514) and an oscilloscope (Tektronix, DPO 2014B). During dialysis, all dialysate was collected and the levofloxacin content was determined by fluorescence spectrometry [29]. A linear correlation was established between the fluorescence intensity (y) and levofloxacin concentration (x, 0.5–2.5 μg/ml), expressed as y = 216.6x + 39.3, R2 = 0.992. The dialysate underwent a tenfold dilution, enabling the determination of levofloxacin content through detection and calculation. The effect of humidity on electrical output was tested in a closed acrylic box containing a humidifier, a dehumidifier, a hygrometer, and a motor-driven TENG output device. First, we used the dehumidifier to reduce the humidity to 20 %, and then we turned on the humidifier and started to record the change of the voltage with the humidity at the output frequency of 4 Hz. A Universal Testing Machine (UTM, Chuanbai, WDW-5Y) was used to test the tensile property of the triboelectric electrode (Tensile mode, 10 mm/s) and the mechanical strength of the LevMN patch (Compact model, 2 mm/min). The volumetric conductivity (σ = l/RA) of the LevMN was calculated using an effective length (l = 1.8 mm) and effective cross-sectional area (A = 2.25 cm2). The length was measured using a digital microscope (BX53, Olympus, Japan). The resistance was tested by an electrochemical workstation (CHI660E, Chenhua, China). The intersection of the Nyquist plots with the x-axis represents the volume resistance of LevMN (R) [30,31].

As shown in Fig. S19A, the needles side of LevMN were passed through PE membranes and submerged in the PBS of the self-made Franz diffusion cell. The highly conductive silver fabric was placed on LevMN and connected to TENG driven by a crank-link mechanism (5 V, 2 Hz), and an aluminum wire was inserted into the PBS solution and grounded. During the drug release process, the magnetic rotor kept stirring to ensure that the removed LevCDs were homogeneously distributed in the solution. We removed 300 µL of the solution with a syringe at predetermined times and examined it using a UV spectrophotometer (Agilent, Cary 100, Santa Clara, CA, USA) at 260 nm. Then, 300 µL new PBS was added to the system. The process was conducted for 24 h. For each group, there were three parallel trials.

Similar to the hemolysis assay, samples were sterilized by UV irradiation for 30 min and extracted with the complete medium in a cell incubator (37 °C, 5 % CO2) for 24 h at a ratio of 0.2 g/mL. A complete medium containing 100 μg/mL LevCDs was used for the LevCDs group. Then, NIH-3 T3 cells were inoculated into 96-well plates at the density of 3000 cells/well. After incubation in the cell incubator for 6 h waiting for the attachment of cells, the cells were treated with TENG (5 V, 2 Hz) for 2 h each day and placed in the cell incubator. Cell viability was assessed using the CCK-8 method every 24 h over three days.

The extracted liquid of LevMN was obtained using stroke-physiological saline solution (SPSS) as the extraction medium at a ratio of 0.2 g/mL for 24 h at 37 °C. The red blood cells (RBCs) were suspended in the extracted liquid (5 %v/v) and connected to TENG (5 V, 2 Hz) for 1 h as the SepMN group. The RBCs were suspended in DI water and SPSS as positive control and negative control groups, respectively. After centrifugation, the absorbance of the supernatant at 576 nm was measured by a UV spectrophotometer.

NIH-3 T3 cells at the density of 3000 cells/well were inoculated into 96-well plates with a complete medium containing 100 μg/mL LevCDs. After incubation in the cell incubator for 6 h waiting for the attachment of cells, insulated electrodes were suspended parallelly in the complete medium and connected to TENG (5 V, 2 Hz) for 16 h. Then, the CCK-8 assay was performed by examining the absorbance at 450 nm using an enzyme marker.

A complete medium containing 100 μg/mL LevCDs was used to culture NIH 3 T3 cells. When the culture dish (35 mm) was filled with cells, a scratch of about 700 μm was made with the pipette tip. The electrodes of TENG (5 V, 2 Hz) adhered to the bottom of the dish at 1 mm intervals, in which the direction of the electrical field aligned with the direction of cell migration. After incubation for 16 h, the microscope was used to take pictures of the migrating cells, and the migration rate was determined.

NIH 3 T3 cells were inoculated into 96-well plates at a density of 5000 cells/well. The bacterial suspension (20 μL, 106 CFU/mL) was added to the wells. LevMN was cut into rectangular blocks of 2 × 10 mm and suspended in the cell culture medium. The TENG (5 V, 2 Hz) was connected to the LevMN by an acupuncture pin and treated the cell culture medium for two hours. After being cultured in the cell incubator for 16 h, the growth of cells and bacteria was observed with a microscope.

First, E. coli and S. aureus were cultured in LB medium at 37 °C in a shaker, and bacteria in the logarithmic growth phase were used for the experiment. Then, SPSS was used to wash and adjust the concentration of bacteria (106 CFU/mL). Then, the recut LevMN patch by a puncher (diameter: 6 mm) sealed with resin except for the needle side was connected to the TENG (5 V, 2 Hz) for 2 h, as shown in Fig. S17A. Next, the treated bacteria suspension (100 µL) was spread on an LB agar plate. Then it was incubated at 37 °C in a bacteriological incubator (Thermo Fisher) for 24 h. The number of surviving colonies was counted manually.

The bacterial suspension (500 µL, 106 CFU/mL) was spread uniformly on empty LB agar plates. The recut LevMN patch (diameter: 6 mm) was placed on E. coli or S. aureus inoculated agar plates and connected to the working TENG (5 V, 2 Hz) for 2 h, as shown in Fig. S17B. The plates were then incubated at 37 °C in a bacteriological incubator for 24 h and the size of the inhibition zone was measured by Image J.

Initially, a blend of S. aureus (900 μL, 1 × 108 CFU/mL) and DCFH-DA (10 μM) was assembled in sterile tubes, followed by an incubation period of 30 min at 37 °C within a shaker. Subsequently, the solution of LevCDs (100 μL, 1 mg/mL) was incorporated into the preceding mixture, and subjected to a TENG treatment (5 V, 2 Hz) for two hours. In the control groups, either SPSS or H2O2 (1 mM) was added instead of the LevCDs, functioning as negative and positive controls, respectively. Lastly, the fluorescence intensity of DCF at 525 nm was evaluated using a fluorescence spectrophotometer with an excitation of 488 nm. A direct correlation was observed between the ROS content and the fluorescence intensity of DCF.

To the first well of a 96-well plate, add 150 μL of LB liquid medium with LevCDs at a concentration of 60 μg/mL. Perform a doubling dilution using LB liquid medium up to the 10th well. For wells 1 through 10, introduce 50 μL of a bacterial suspension with a concentration of 2 × 106 CFU/mL. Subsequently, incubate these wells at 37 °C for 18 h in a bacterial incubator. After incubation, determine the MIC visually under directed light conditions.

The animal experiment was approved by the Ethics Committee of the Life Science Center of Harbin Institute of Technology (No. IACUC-2023056). All animal experiments conformed to animal welfare standards. Ten-week-old male Balb/c mice were randomly assigned to CTRL, MN, LevMN, LevMNe, and SepMN groups, with three mice in each group. Mice were fed a standard diet with 12 h of light and 12 h of darkness in the housing environment. Wound models of full-thickness wounds were established using a puncher (diameter: 6 mm) and the wound was infected with S. aureus suspension (20 μL, 108 CFU/mL). The LevMN was placed on the wound, connected to the TENG driven by a crank-link mechanism (5 V, 2 Hz), and bound up with Tegaderm Film (3 M, 1626 W) and bandage. The LevMN patch was changed every three days, while the electrical stimulation was applied for 24 h each time. Mice were euthanized on day 12, and tissues were harvested and fixed overnight in a 4 % paraformaldehyde solution. Tissue sections were conducted histological analyses including hematoxylin-eosin (H&E) and Masson’s trichrome. Image J software was used to quantify and analyze the tissue section.

Experimental data were expressed as standard deviation ± mean. All experiments were performed in triplicate. The t-test was used to determine differences between the experiment group and other groups, and statistically significant differences are indicated by *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, and ***p < 0.001.

For practical application, the wearable TENG component of the SepMN system is situated at a mobile part of the body, whereas the LevMN is applied to the infected wound; these two components are interconnected via wires, as illustrated in Fig. 1 A. The FTE can generate triboelectricity efficiently with most common clothing fabrics in single-electrode mode. It was fabricated from Mxene nanosheets, alginate, and glycerol via a film-casting method, and its morphology is depicted in Fig. S1. The electrical stimulation generated from the FTE is subsequently transmitted through a wire to a highly conductive silver fabric (Fig. S2), which then relays the electrical stimulation to the LevMN, as shown in Fig. 1B. The LevCDs within the microneedle patch exhibit dual functionality: electrical conductivity and bacteriostatic properties. This is achieved via a drug-design paradigm that bonded the carboxyl group of levofloxacin with the amino group of amino-functionalized carbon quantum dots through an amide reaction. As shown in Fig. 1Ci and S3, the resultant LevCDs are nanoparticles with a relatively uniform size distribution in the range of 2–10 nm. Notably, lattice stripes are apparent in the LevCDs, featuring plane spacings of 0.24 nm (Fig. 1Cii), corresponding to the lattice stripes of carbon quantum dots [32]. The microneedle patch loaded with LevCDs comprises a 20 × 20 pyramidal array, which fluoresces cyan under UV illumination (Fig. 1D, S4).

Mice models with infected wounds are used to validate the therapeutic efficacy of the system (Fig. 1E). During treatment, LevCDs generate reactive oxygen species (ROS), contributing to sterilization. In parallel, electrical stimulation promotes cell proliferation, cell migration, and collagen deposition (Fig. 1F). Consequently, the SepMN system enables accelerated infected wound healing through antimicrobial treatment and electrically stimulated tissue repair.

Carbon quantum dots are ideal precursors for the synthesis of conductive drugs owing to their good electrical conductivity, biocompatibility, and small size (1–10 nm) [33,34]. Levofloxacin—a broad-spectrum antibiotic—has a variety of functional groups, such as ether, ketone, and carboxyl groups, which impart chemical reactivity [35]. We propose a drug-design strategy to endow the drug with dual functionality: electrical stimulation delivery and bactericidal capabilities. Specifically, amino-functionalized carbon quantum dots (AnCDs) were first synthesized through a one-step hydrothermal method using glucose, ammonia, and hydrogen peroxide as raw materials. Subsequently, levofloxacin was activated by EDC/NHS in a 37 °C water bath for 30 min, after which AnCDs were added and reacted for 24 h to obtain LevCDs (as illustrated in Fig. 2 A). The aqueous solutions of AnCDs and LevCDs exhibit prominent blue and cyan fluorescence under UV light, respectively (Fig. S5). Fig. 2B illustrates the high electrical conductivity of LevCDs, which reaches about 3.1 mS/cm at a concentration of 3 mg/mL. A series of tests were conducted to confirm the successful fabrication of LevCDs.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images reveal that the LevCDs have an average diameter size of approximately 6.18 nm (Fig. 1C, S3), and are larger than the original AnCDs (Fig. S6). After the reaction, the monodispersity of the LevCDs is lower than that of the AnCDs, yet the lattice distance remains constant at 0.24 nm. This observation suggests that levofloxacin is attached to the surface of the carbon quantum dots without altering their internal structure. Further structural insights into LevCDs were gained from Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy measurements (Fig. 2C), which reveal the presence of amide groups through C = O (1628 cm−1), C–N (1402 cm−1) stretching, and N-H (1557 cm−1) bending [36]. Moreover, the ether bond and benzene ring of LevCDs, which are characteristic peaks of levofloxacin (Fig. S7), are identified in the ranges of 1050-1330 cm−1 and 1400–1600 cm−1, respectively. These findings confirm the formation of amide bonds between the amino groups of the carbon quantum dots and the carboxyl group of levofloxacin. Fig. 2D presents the results of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis, which was conducted to further understand the chemical structure of the LevCDs. A binding energy of 399.38 eV, accounting for 44.64 % in the detailed N 1 s spectrum of AnCDs, is assigned to the C-NH2 bond. Detailed spectral analysis for LevCDs suggests possible chemical interaction in the N-related functional groups, given the higher binding energy of N than that of AnCDs. The binding energy of 399.43 eV, which corresponded to 69.85 % of the composition, is attributed to the amide bond, –CO-NH-, signifying the successful conversion of C-NH2 to –CO-NH- through chemical treatment.

The fluorescence properties of AnCDs and LevCDs were explored through fluorescence-excitation spectroscopy. As the excitation wavelength increased, the AnCDs emission peaks gradually redshift and show excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior, whereas the LevCDs emission peaks remain constant (Fig. 2E). This difference in fluorescence properties could be attributed to the different functional groups present on the surface of AnCDs after levofloxacin conjugation [14,33]. All the results strongly validate the successful synthesis of LevCDs. Besides, the loaded content of levofloxacin within the LevCDs was quantified as about 12 % by comparing the total amount of input levofloxacin and the unconjugated levofloxacin in the dialysate. The UV-absorption spectrum of LevCDs shows a peak at approximately 260 nm, and a high linear correlation is established between the absorbance and LevCDs concentration (Fig. S8).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and output performance of the TENG
ce:section.ce:para: The SepMN system incorporates the FTE as a component of the wearable TENG. The FTE has an opaque, black appearance, and excellent tensile properties, as depicted in Fig. 3 A. Notably, its stretchability reaches a maximum elongation of 63.9 % (Fig. S9). Given that the peak strain for a human limb is 27 %, the FTE design sufficiently addresses the requirements for practical application [37].

The LevCDs-loaded microneedle patch (LevMN) was manufactured using a template method, as shown in Fig. S10. The LevMN withstands a compressive force up to approximately 0.26 N per needle without breaking (Fig. 3B), surpassing the previously reported cuticle penetration minimum force requirement of 0.045 N [38,39]. In addition, the ability of LevMN to penetrate the skin was evident from the formation of a distinct spot array where LevMN was applied to the skin of mice using finger pressure, as illustrated in the inset image of Fig. 3B.

To ascertain the triboelectric potential of FTE with common clothing fabrics, we fabricated TENGs using FTEs with 10 randomly selected fabrics. The output performance of each group was evaluated by gauging the voltage output at 4 Hz in single-electrode mode. Our investigation reveals that all 10 fabrics generate electrical stimulation with the FTE; however, the electrical output performances vary among the different fabrics. Based on the voltage output measurements, six fabrics are classified as high-performance (Fig. 3C): tropical suiting, knitting wool, polyester fiber, polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) fiberglass, imitation silk, and acetate fiber, all of which generated voltages exceeding 50 V, with cellulose acetate peaking at 120 V. By contrast, four fabric types—alpaca wool, doupioni pongee, DuPont paper, and mercerized cotton—are identified as low-performance owing to their voltage output, which fluctuates between 10 and 20 V (Fig. 3D). As detailed in Tables S1 and S2, the composition and surface morphology of the fabrics strongly influenced the output performance of the TENG. For example, although both common polyester fiber and imitation silk are composed entirely of polyester, the compact structure of the latter, which enhances the contact area, results in a higher output. Nonetheless, the fabric composition is a crucial determinant of the electrical output performance of the TENG. High-output fabrics included wool, polyester, PTFE fiberglass, and acetate fiber, which are commonly used in the textile industry. To further examine the electrical performance of the FTE, a TENG was constructed with PTFE fiberglass because of its high electrical output. Upon in vivo application, the TENG generated effective electrical stimulation induced by the mice’s motions, as shown in Video S1.

The electrical output is generated by a synergy of contact charging and electrostatic induction, as depicted in Fig. 3E. Specifically, FTE and PTFE surfaces generate positive and negative charges, respectively, owing to the higher triboelectric positivity of the FTE. The FTE and conductive silver fabric are linked via a wire, which allows them to act as a whole conductor. As such, electrons transfer from the silver fabric to the FTE when the FTE and PTFE fiberglass are separated, yielding a positive charge on the surfaces of both the FTE and silver fabric. Subsequently, the LevCDs in the microneedle patch transport the electrical stimulation to the wound tissue. When the PTFE re-approached the FTE, electron transport commenced in the reverse direction. Therefore, through cycles of contact and separation, the TENG continuously supplied electrical stimulation to the wound site. As depicted in Figure S11, the electrical output of the TENG decreases with an increase in relative humidity. Nevertheless, the voltage remains 42 V at the relative humidity of 80 %. This trend aligns with the general pattern illustrating the effect of humidity on TENG output. The electrical stimulation voltage required for wound treatment is generally less than 10 V [13,17,20]. Therefore, it is feasible to meet the voltage required for wound treatment in most human habitats. The electrical output performance of the TENG was systematically characterized in an environment with a relative humidity of about 45 %. Besides, the output performance of the TENG can also be influenced by the contact frequency in our experiments. As the contact frequency is increased from 1 to 4 Hz, the voltage output increases from 70 to 130 V (Fig. 3F), and the current output increases from 2 to 6 μA (Fig. 3G). This effect is ascribable to the increase in momentum at higher contact frequencies, which deforms the friction layer [40,41]. This deformation potentially enlarges the effective contact area of the friction material, thereby enhancing the output performance (Fig. S12). Fig. 3H shows the TENG output performance at 4 Hz using resistors of varying external load. The voltage increases with increasing load resistance, whereas the current decreases. The TENG’s stability and reliability are confirmed by testing for 400,000 operating cycles, as shown in Fig. 3I. By integrating a bridge-rectifier circuit, the TENG charges capacitors of differing capacitance, highlighting its potential as an energy source for electronic devices (Fig. S13). In addition, the volumetric conductivity of LevMN increased with the concentration of loaded LevCDs. Compared to the microneedle patch without LevCDs (0.093 mS/cm), LevMN exhibited a conductivity of 0.227 mS/cm at a concentration of 3 mg/mL LevCDs (Fig. S14). With LevMN as the circuit wiring and a TENG comprising an FTE and PTFE fiberglass as the micro-power source, the system illuminates 60 LEDs in single-electrode mode (Fig. 3J and Video S2). This demonstrates the excellent electrical conductivity of the LevMN and the robust electrical output of the TENG.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biocompatibility and cellular responses subjected to SepMN system
ce:section.ce:para: The biocompatibility of the LevCDs was evaluated by CCK8 assay. NIH 3 T3 cells were cultured using the extract liquid of LevMN containing different LevCDs content, it was found that cell viability was minimally affected by the LevCDs content between 0 and 2 mg/ml. Notably, upon surpassing 2 mg/ml, there is a decline in cell viability (Fig. S15). Considering the factors of biocompatibility and electrical conductivity, we incorporate 2 mg/ml of LevCDs into the microneedle patches for the subsequent experiment. The cytotoxicity of the SepMN system was explored thoroughly. After electrical stimulation for 2 h, over 85 % of NIH3T3 cells survive in the extracted liquid of the different samples on the first day, with a notable upsurge in cell proliferation within 3 days (Fig. 4 A). The staining results indicate that cells in all the groups are in good condition with green coloration and spindle shapes (Fig. 4B). Furthermore, we performed hemocompatibility tests by introducing red blood cells (RBCs) to the liquid extract of LevMN following electrical stimulation. Both SepMN and other groups demonstrate less than 5 % hemolytic activity (Fig. 4C). Additionally, optical imaging of RBCs after treatment shows round morphology in the experimental groups. In contrast, RBCs treated with deionized (DI) water do not survive, as only fragments of ruptured RBCs are observed in the microscope (Fig. 4D). These results indicate that the SepMN system has a substantial hemocompatibility with RBCs. These findings suggest that SepMN displays superior biocompatibility, making it suitable for wound-site application. In addition, the cellular behavior under electrical stimulation was evaluated through cell migration and proliferation studies. As depicted in Fig. 4E, electrical stimulation markedly increased the proliferation of NIH3T3 cells after a 16-h incubation period. Besides, the cell migration rate in the LevCDs/TENG groups is higher than in the control and LevCDs groups (Fig. 4F and G). This result indicates that electrical stimulation can effectively promote cell proliferation and migration.

Bacterial infections are a significant factor that can impede wound healing. Therefore, treatment for wounds should involve bacterial suppression or sterilization to prevent infection. In this study, we investigated the antibacterial properties of the SepMN system using Escherichia coli (E. coli, Gram-negative) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus, Gram-positive).

We conducted a bacterial-invasion experiment using the TENG at a voltage of 5 V at 2 Hz (Fig. S16). The results reveal dense cell growth without any bacterial proliferation in both the LevMN and SepMN groups (Fig. 5 A). By contrast, the CTRL, MN, and TENG groups demonstrate quick bacterial proliferation, which impedes cell growth. Besides, the survival rate of bacteria after treatment with electrical stimulation was almost unchanged (Fig. 5B). These results indicate that electrical stimulation alone provides limited bacterial eradication, whereas both LevMN and SepMN effectively safeguard cells against bacterial intrusion. We quantified colony-forming units (CFUs) experiments to further investigate the antibacterial activity of LevMN and SepMN (Fig. S17A). As shown in Fig. 5C-E, the LevMN group displays a significantly lower number of residual E. coli and S. aureus colonies on the agar plates than the CTRL and MN group. Notably, the SepMN group nearly eradicates all bacterial colonies. Besides, the antibacterial efficiency of SepMN was assessed by measuring the diameter of the inhibition zone (Fig. S17B), which is directly related to the antibacterial activity. In contrast with the CTRL and MN groups, both the LevMN and SepMN have discernible inhibitory zones (Fig. 5F). The diameters of the inhibitory circles for E. coli and S. aureus are 1.30 and 1.62 mm larger, respectively, for SepMN than the LevMN (Fig. 5G). These results confirm that the LevCDs have excellent antibacterial properties and that electrical stimulation augments the bactericidal ability of LevMN. This enhancement can be attributed to electrical stimulation facilitating the release of LevCDs, thereby increasing LevCDs exposure to the bacteria.

The ROS induced by the SepMN system were detected indirectly by the conversion of 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein diacetate to 2′,7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein [42,43]. Our study indicates that LevCDs can proficiently stimulate the generation of intracellular ROS within bacterial cells (Fig. S18), which is consistent with previous reports of carbon quantum dots inducing bacterial intracellular ROS [44]. Notably, the relative concentration of ROS engendered by LevCDs surpasses that of H2O2. Prior studies suggested that TENG could induce the production of intracellular ROS, but we were unable to detect ROS, potentially because of the brief duration of the electrical stimulation.

The minimal inhibitory concentration (MIC) for LevCDs against S. aureus and E. coli was determined to be 1.875 μg/mL and 0.469 μg/mL, respectively. To ensure effective electrical stimulation delivery and comprehensive sterilization, microneedles are loaded with adequate amounts of the conductive sterilization LevCDs (2 mg/mL) in LevMN and SepMN groups. The dosage used in microneedles surpasses the MIC for bacteria substantially. Therefore, despite variations in the release of LevCDs, both the LevMN and SepMN groups may fulfill bactericidal requirements owing to the sufficient loading of LevCDs in practical application. An in-depth discussion will be presented in the in vivo study section.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo evaluation of wound healing
ce:section.ce:para: The practical application of SepMN was further explored using mice models of infected wounds (Fig. 6 A). In the antibacterial experiments above, the electrical stimulation of TENG demonstrated limited bactericidal capacity. Consequently, it was deemed unsuitable for treating infected wounds and the TENG group was not included in the in vivo study. First, a full-thickness wound was created on the back of mice using a 6-mm puncher and then inoculated with S. aureus. Subsequently, the mice were randomly assigned to CTRL, MN, LevMN, and SepMN groups (Fig. 6B). The wound was recorded at regular intervals from days 1 to 12. The dynamic changes in wound morphology reveal that the LevMN and SepMN groups exhibit faster wound healing and a less-pronounced inflammatory response than the other groups (Fig. 6C). Quantitative analysis indicates that the relative wound areas in the CTRL, MN, LevMN, and SepMN groups decreased to 16.5 %± 3.0 %, 18.2 % ± 2.8 %, 10.3 %± 1.8 %, and 4.4 %±0.9 % within 12 days, respectively (Fig. 6D). These findings indicate that blank microneedle has no therapeutic effects on infected wounds. When LevCDs are incorporated into the microneedles, the LevCDs eliminate the bacteria, leading to a reduction in inflammation and an increased healing rate in the LevMN group as opposed to the CTRL and MN groups. The application of electrical stimulation further enhances the wound healing rate of the SepMN group compared to the LevMN group. However, the SepMN group has two variables relative to the LevMN group, which include more released LevCDs and electrical stimulation. The release of LevCDs from the SepMN system was studied using a self-made Franz diffusion cell, as shown in Fig. S19A. The results show that the passive release of LevCDs from LevMN is constant, with a cumulative release rate of 49.63 % at 24 h. After applying electrical stimulation to the LevMN, the rate of LevCDs release reaches 59.54 % (Fig. S19B). The passive release of LevCDs is due to the principle of substance diffusion, which always diffuses from a place of high concentration to a place of low concentration[45,46]. The LevCDs is a negatively charged nanoparticle due to its Zeta potential being determined as −3.42 mV. The increase in the release of LevCDs under the electric field generated by triboelectric nanogenerators may be attributed to the enhanced mobility of charged substances under the electric field, which affects the drug release kinetics[20,47–49].

To identify whether the better treatment outcome of the SepMN group relative to the LevMN group is due to the more released of LevCDs or electrical stimulation, we developed a microneedle patch (denoted LevMNe) without electrical stimulation owning a LevCDs-release amount similar to that of the SepMN group. This was achieved by elevating the concentration of LevCDs in the microneedle patch. The LevCDs concentrations were 2 mg/mL in both LevMN and SepMN groups, and 2.8 mg/mL in the LevMNe group. Correspondingly, the LevCDs release amounts are approximately 300 μg in the LevMN group and 360 μg in both the SepMN and LevMNe groups (Fig. S20A). The results suggest that the relative wound area and the wound healing process in the LevMNe group are comparable to those in the LevMN group (Fig. S20B and 6C). This result can be attributed to the released LevCDs dosage of both LevMN and LevMNe groups eradicating bacteria thoroughly, and the more released LevCDs in the LevMNe group do not further promote wound healing. Additionally, it is observed that single-application LevCDs do not foster cell proliferation and migration (Fig. 4 E-G). Although the LevCDs exhibited excellent antibacterial properties, they have limited capacity to promote wound healing directly. Consequently, the superior treatment effects of the SepMN group to the LevMN group can be attributed to the electrically stimulated wound repair.

Histological analyses were performed to evaluate wound healing on day 12 (Fig. 6E). The results of the hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining indicate that the CTRL and MN groups have a thick, newly formed epidermis, enlarged width of granulation tissue, and a low number of skin appendages including hair follicles and sebaceous glands. By contrast, the LevMN and SepMN groups display complete restoration of the epidermis, reduced width of granulation tissue and epithelial tissue thickness, increased new hair follicles, and a significant reduction in neutrophil and lymphocyte infiltration (Fig. S21). Masson’s trichrome staining shows that the SepMN group exhibited more mature collagen deposition than the other groups. The quantitative analysis illustrates that the SepMN group has the best healing conditions, with a granulation tissue width of 738.5 ± 96.9 μm (Fig. 6F) and an epithelial thickness of 39.8 ± 6.0 μm, which is comparable to the thickness of normal mouse epithelial tissue (21.3 ± 0.9 μm) (Fig. 6G). H&E staining of main mice organs reveals no pathological damage with SepMN treatment. Thus, these results suggest that the SepMN system is highly biocompatible (Fig. S22). In addition, considering that the electrical output of the TENG does not have fixed frequencies and voltages in practical applications, the effects of the random electrical output applied to the LevMN on cells, bacteria, and mice further validate the feasibility of this SepMN system for the practical treatment of infected wounds (Fig. S23, Video S1, and Note S1).

In summary, we developed a wearable, self-powered microneedle system based on conductive drugs to treat infected wounds. The separation of the wearable TENG of the system from the drug-loaded microneedle patch makes it possible to easily collect the triboelectricity generated from easy-to-move areas, such as the elbow joint. The synthesized LevCDs exhibited high bacteriostasis and good conductivity in the SepMN system. Additionally, wires and silver fabric were used to transfer the electrical stimulation produced from the TENG to the drug-loaded microneedle patch. In vitro experiments showed that electrical stimulation promoted the degree of cell activation, including migration and proliferation. In in vivo studies, the SepMN system effectively eliminated inflammatory responses and accelerated wound closure. This advanced SepMN system has no complex components and low manufacturing costs. Therefore, this work provides a new strategy for the treatment of infected wounds and promotes the development of wearable wound-care-related equipment.

H.S., and H.W. analyzed the data. W.W., N.Z., and M.A. participated in the discussion and data interpretation. All the authors contributed extensively to the work presented in this paper.
